did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an intelligent Content-Based Dermoscopic Image Retrieval (CBDIR) system with Relevance Feedback for melanoma diagnosis. The AI methods include Linear Kernel-based active Support Vector Machines (LKSVMactive) for classification and active learning sampling, combined with histogram intersection-based similarity measures for feature retrieval. The system extracts color and texture features (72-dimensional feature vectors) from dermoscopic images to classify skin lesions as melanoma or benign.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel intelligent Content-Based Dermoscopic Image Retrieval (CBDIR) system with Relevance Feedback for melanoma diagnosis. It details the development of a specific algorithm using Linear Kernel-based active Support Vector Machines (LKSVMactive) and histogram intersection similarity measures. The authors present original experimental results, including performance metrics (accuracy, MAP, MAR) and comparisons against other systems (S1, S2, S3) on a 1097 image database, which constitutes original technical research rather than a literature review.

title: Intelligent Content-Based Dermoscopic Image Retrieval with Relevance Feedback for Computer-Aided Melanoma Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study proposes an intelligent Content-Based Dermoscopic Image Retrieval (CBDIR) system with Relevance Feedback to assist in computer-aided melanoma diagnosis. The system utilizes Linear Kernel-based active Support Vector Machines (LKSVMactive) and histogram intersection similarity measures to retrieve relevant skin lesion images from a database of 1097 dermoscopic images. By leveraging active learning and user feedback, the approach aims to bridge the semantic gap inherent in classical CBIR systems. The diagnosis for a query image is determined by the dominant class (melanoma or benign) among the retrieved most similar images. Extensive experiments demonstrated that the proposed scheme is more effective than CBDIR systems without relevance feedback assistance.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the 1097 image database; 88 confirmed malignant cases (melanomas) were included. Used as the positive class for diagnosis.
One of the two classes in the 1097 image database; 1009 benign lesions were included. The paper notes images were annotated as 'melanoma/nexus'. Used as the negative class for diagnosis.

all_task_types.details: The system uses a Linear Kernel-based active Support Vector Machine (LKSVMactive) to classify skin lesions. The diagnosis for a query image is determined by the dominant class (melanoma or benign) among the retrieved most similar images. The SVM classifier predicts the class (positive or negative) of the training set image, and the final query diagnosis is assigned the label of the dominant class in the retrieved set.

all_task_types.target: Most similar dermoscopic images from a database
all_task_types.details: The system performs Content-Based Dermoscopic Image Retrieval (CBDIR) using histogram intersection-based similarity measures to retrieve the K most similar skin lesion images from a database of 1097 images.

datasets_used.name: Wazaefi, Paris & Fertil (2012) Dermoscopic Image Database
datasets_used.description: A database of 1097 dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions, furnished and previously annotated by expert dermatologists using a Heine Delta 20 dermoscope equipped with a SONY W120 digital camera.

use.training: A subset of 140 images (70 melanoma, 70 benign) was randomly selected from the parent database to build the training set for the SVM classifier.
use.testing: 30 images were randomly selected from the remaining database to serve as test queries for evaluating the retrieval system.
use.other: The remaining images (1097 - 140 - 30 = 927) formed the test set/database from which relevant images were retrieved during the experiment.

datasets_used.name: Web-collected Macroscopic Skin Lesion Images
datasets_used.description: A set of macroscopic skin lesion images collected from the web, characterized by different sizes, varied types of artifacts, and different illumination conditions and contrast.

use.testing: Used to test the approach's ability to handle non-dermoscopic images and remove occlusions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classifier using linear, polynomial, Gaussian radial basis, and histogram intersection kernels.
To measure the retrieval performance of the CBDIR system with relevance feedback.
To measure the retrieval performance of the CBDIR system with relevance feedback.
To evaluate the retrieval system performance.
To evaluate the retrieval system performance.
To report the variability of Average Precision (AP) and Average Recall (AR) statistics across the proposed approach compared to systems S1 and S2.
To compare the distribution of AP and AR values for the proposed approach against systems S1 and S2.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics such as accuracy, Mean Average Precision (MAP), Mean Average Recall (MAR), standard deviation, min, best, median, and mean values.

Wazaefi, Paris & Fertil (2012) Dermoscop.notes: The linear kernel yielded the best classification accuracy compared to polynomial, radial basis, and histogram intersection kernels. It also had faster training/classification speed and less memory requirements. The system was tested with three rounds of relevance feedback. MAP and MAR were measured but specific numerical values for these retrieval metrics across different K values or feedback rounds were not explicitly reported in the provided text, only the formulas and the claim that the proposed scheme is more effective than CBDIR without RF.

Proposed CBDIR with RF System.performance: Successfully handled images, removed occlusions (hair, ruler marking, black frames), and extracted skin lesion regions.

Web-collected Macroscopic Skin Lesion Im.notes: The approach was tested on macroscopic images characterized by different sizes, varied artifacts, and different illumination/contrast. Results showed the system could handle these non-dermoscopic images effectively.

architecture_used.main_model: Linear Kernel-based active Support Vector Machine (LKSVMactive)
architecture_used.input_size: 72-dimensional feature vector (derived from color and texture features)

feature_extraction.color_features: Quantization into 7 bins, HSV space conversion, mean/variance per bin, Gaussian distribution statistics (average means, average variance, spreadness, elongation)
feature_extraction.texture_features: Haar wavelet transform (4 sub-images), Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) on sub-images (energy mean, variance, spreadness, elongation)

training_details.active_learning_strategy: Selects closest images to the SVM hyperplane for feedback

classification_or_output_head.decision_logic: Dominant class among the retrieved K most similar images

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Linear Kernel-based active Support Vector Machine (LKSVMactive)
preprocessing_techniques: Artifact disocclusion using Dullrazor software to remove hair and ruler markings
Black frame removal using circular masks with different diameters
Skin lesion segmentation using active contour model (Chan & Vese) and Otsu thresholding
Application of morphological operations to obtain skin lesion color mask
Feature normalization (color and texture descriptors normalized before SVM training)
conclusions: The proposed intelligent Content-Based Dermoscopic Image Retrieval (CBDIR) system with Relevance Feedback (RF) is more effective than CBDIR systems without RF assistance.
The system utilizes Linear Kernel-based active Support Vector Machines (LKSVMactive) combined with histogram intersection-based similarity measures to retrieve relevant skin lesion images.

The linear kernel SVM achieved the best classification accuracy of 92.5% compared to polynomial, Gaussian radial basis, and histogram intersection kernels, while also offering faster training/classification speed and lower memory requirements.

Active learning sampling significantly improved Mean Average Precision (MAP) values in the second and third feedback rounds (72.66% and 73.05% respectively), indicating the importance of active learning in the retrieval process.
The proposed method achieved the best Mean Average Recall (MAR) values in the second feedback round, demonstrating that it can effectively retrieve relevant images with fewer iterations.
The system successfully handles both dermoscopic and macroscopic skin lesion images, effectively removing occlusions such as hair, ruler markings, and black frames.

The diagnosis for a query image is determined by the dominant class (melanoma or benign) among the retrieved most similar images, resulting in an overall classification error of 21.28% across 30 test queries.
The system bridges the semantic gap inherent in classical CBIR systems by allowing user interaction through passive and active feedback modes to refine retrieval results.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, highly imbalanced dataset (1097 images total, with only 88 melanoma cases) and a very small test set of 30 images, limiting statistical power and generalizability.

The training set was artificially balanced (70 melanoma, 70 benign) despite the natural class imbalance in the source database (88 melanoma vs. 1009 benign), which may overestimate performance on real-world clinical data.
The system was evaluated on a single, specific database (Wazaefi, Paris & Fertil) without external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about dataset bias.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; comparisons against baseline systems (S1, S2, S3) relied on descriptive statistics (mean, median, standard deviation).
The diagnosis logic depends on the 'dominant class' among retrieved images, resulting in a high classification error rate of 21.28% on the test queries, which is significant for a diagnostic aid.

The system's effectiveness is heavily dependent on user interaction (Relevance Feedback); performance without RF (System S1) was substantially lower, indicating limited utility for fully automated, non-interactive deployment.
The paper does not report confidence intervals for the reported accuracy (92.5%) or retrieval metrics (MAP/MAR), making it difficult to assess the precision of these estimates.
The source of the dermoscopic images and the specific criteria for expert annotation are not detailed in the provided context, leaving potential annotation bias unaddressed.

The system was tested on macroscopic images with artifacts (hair, rulers) to demonstrate robustness, but these were qualitative results rather than quantitative metrics, limiting the evidence for real-world robustness.
